title: Unlocking the Brain's Potential - Robert Langer
text:
I would now like to introduce Bob
Langanger. Bob is the uh institute
professor and he's been a board member
of the McGovern Institute from the very
beginning. Uh he has been a a a source
of wisdom and encouragement uh all these
years. I always look forward to when Bob
Langanger joins our board meeting and
he's going to give us the um keynote
lecture today on uh it's some sort of
journey the slides up.
Thank you.
Well, thank you very much. Is it uh
sound? Thank you very much. Um, you
know, I I you got to hear some
outstanding music before and you know
that may not exactly fit in with
neuroscience, but it was terrific. I'm
now you're going to hear something from
an engineer and I who knows Phil said
maybe he doesn't know neuroscience.
Well, believe me, I know it a lot less,
but I um but I'm honored really to have
been a part of the board for many years
and uh well, since the beginning and I
and so I thought I'd tell you a little
bit about my own journey at MIT. um you
know which is is different but Bob
mentioned to me about the possibility of
talking about how research can lead to
uh collaboration on the one hand but
also how research can lead to things
that maybe can affect uh you know people
around the world and so I'll try to do
that as well but before I do I thought I
would uh say a few words about some
people you know I must have been about
1980 I was uh consulting uh for a
company in California in PaloAlto and
they said, "Bob, there's this uh dinner
speaker that we all want to take you to
and listen to." And he he actually makes
these computer magazines. And that was
Pat McGovern. And I remember, you know,
he he had uh been talking about uh it
was really amazing how incredibly
successful he was and the enormous
influence that he had. So that was the
first time I' I'd heard that. And you
know, of course, of I as I followed his
remarkable career and actually Lor's
remarkable career over the years, it was
um you know, just a pleasure to watch
that and really what they did for MIT
and and actually what they did for
neuroscience, you know, the the the gift
that they gave uh it was really
transformational and it really I think
forever changed MIT's position and
actually the kind of science that could
be done here and and really changed the
world in terms of of neuroscience. It's
just been remarkable to see what's
happened uh you know since since the
McGovern Institute was founded and
you'll hear that more and more this
afternoon and really between and and of
course both of them were on the board
and Lori still has it's amazing you know
I I I love being on the board to listen
to the really her comments. I mean, she
she certainly is not going to be shy
about uh giving opinions and and and
really it's made a giant difference.
I also uh actually have this picture,
you know, and you just heard from Phil,
but just as as again an outsider, you
know, as I've watched been at MIT myself
now as a graduate student uh and and a
professor for actually the last it's
hard for me to believe 55 years, I can
think of no individual that has really
made made MIT great more than Phil. I
mean he as both a leader you know of
biology department of the cancer
institute then of the McGovern Institute
and and really just the incredible
advice he's given to everybody at MIT
and the vision he's had been absolutely
remarkable and that and that's separate
from the fact that he's an unbelievable
scientist and has changed the world that
way too and and an incredibly nice guy.
Uh so he really got this all started and
uh you know and and and then actually I
think it was 2004 maybe uh but we were
doing the search and we were so
fortunate to get Bob uh as the head and
he has just done an amazing job. I mean
to watch the growth of what has happened
at the at the McGovern Institute uh and
to see all the tremendous young people
and what's been going on. It's
absolutely remarkable. So I I just it's
really been a privilege to be a
spectator uh on the board. So with that
said, I thought I would tell you a
little bit, you know, and like I say,
very different. It's just my personal
journey uh for for entertainment, I
guess, that you know, so that that I
thought I'd go over and how I got into
this. So I I I got my degree in chemical
engineering in
1974 and when I did uh pretty much all
my colleagues in chemical engineering,
graduate students went to work for oil
companies. So there was this gas
shortage then and uh if you actually
lived in Boston, you had to wait in line
for two hours at a gas station to get
your tank filled. The consequence of
that though is that if you were a
chemical engineer, you got a lot of job
offers. And so that's what all my
friends did. I thought that's what I'd
do, too. So, I applied to oil companies.
I actually got 20 job offers, four from
Exxon
alone. And I wasn't very good, but I
just uh you know, I they'd hire anybody.
So,
um especially if they're from MIT, but
uh but you know, one of those interviews
really made quite an impression on me. I
they had some MIT graduates from 1966 in
Exxon in Baton Rouge, Louisiana. And
they told me, "Gee, if I could just
increase the yield of this one
prochemical by some very tiny
percentage, they said it'd be fantastic.
It'd be worth billions of dollars." And
I remember flying back to Boston that
night thinking to myself, I really
didn't want to do that. And and and in
fact, of all those so I I so I didn't I
didn't do that. And I so I started to
think what I did want to do. And when I
was a graduate student, I was supposed
to be doing my PhD thesis. I got very
involved in starting an alternative high
school in Cambridge for working-class uh
students and and I got very involved in
creating new chemistry curriculum and
one day I saw an ad at City College of
New York to be an assistant professor to
do just that. So I wrote them a letter
applying for a job but but they didn't
write me back. But but I like that idea.
So I found all the ads I could to do
something like that. I found about 40 I
wrote to all these places and I don't
think any of them wrote me back. So that
wasn't going so well. So I started to
think, what other way could I use my
chemical engineering background to help
people? And I thought about medicine. So
I wrote to a lot of hospitals and
medical schools. Uh none of them wrote
me back either. But then one day I was
walking into the lab where I was and one
of the older scientists said to me, he
said, "Bob," he said, "There's a surgeon
in Boston named Judah Folkman." And he
said, "Sometimes he hires unusual
people." He he thought very highly of
Dr. Folkman, but you know, uh, I don't
know exactly what he thought about me,
but any I wrote to Dr. Folkman, and Dr.
Folkman had this idea that if you could
stop blood vessels, that might be a
whole new way to think about treating
cancer. And he also thought that there
was a chemical he called TAFF, tumor
angroenesis factor, that cause these
vessels to grow. Now, it turns out that
people were incredibly skeptical of this
theory. Um, I didn't know any biology. I
I still don't know very much, but I I
thought it was great, you know, and I so
I went to work there. It was by far the
lowest paying job, and I was the only
engineer in Children's Hospital, but I
learned a tremendous amount. Anyhow, he
he said, "Bob, what he wanted me to do
was prove he was right, but in so doing
isolate the first what we call
androgenesis inhibitor." Now, the
biggest problem that we faced at that
time was there was really no way to
study blood vessel growth. And the
reason for that is almost everywhere
you'd look in any organism or person
there's always background blood vessels.
So um so that's not so easy. So we had
to invent an approach and and what we
did there's a certain places like the
chick coral and membrane um or a cornea
that doesn't have blood vessels. So our
idea was maybe we could put certain
tumors in them which would cause blood
vessels to grow and then maybe we'd have
a tiny delivery system like a
microparticle or nano particle that
could deliver the molecules we uh we
wanted to deliver and the molecules we
want to deliver were were pretty big
molecules. So at that time 1974 there
was almost no work going on in advanced
drug delivery systems. In fact there was
only one company in the world then that
was working on them. was a company
called Alza out in California actually
in Palo Alto. Uh and and Dr. Folkman was
on their advisory board and they had
actually Nobel Prize winners in
chemistry on their advisory board and he
asked them could they help us but what
they all said was no. They said by the
way that company would later make
transermal delivery systems for but all
for small molecules but they all said to
him that you really aren't going to be
able to do it because they can't get
through solid materials anymore than
like we could walk through a wall. So on
the other hand, if we were going to
solve the blood vessel problem, we had
to solve this problem. So I actually
spent two years working on this,
experimenting with different materials,
different techniques. I actually found
over 200 different ways to get this to
not
work. But eventually we made these
little tiny particles. You see a picture
of one and one cut in half. And we
published in the British journal Nature
that you could use this to release
molecules of any size. It's also the
first time that nucleic acids which DNA
and RNA are was were delivered from the
tiny particles. So I I want to go back
though to about three months before this
paper came out. I had got asked to give
a talk at a major scientific meeting. I
was 27 years old then 1976 and so I
practiced this talk for weeks into a
tape recorder and finally the day came
and I ga got up and I gave the talk and
you know I was by far the youngest
person there. There were a lot of very
well-known scientists there. And I
thought at the end of the talk, I I
didn't do too badly. I didn't forget too
much of what I was going to say or I
didn't stammer too much. So, I thought
when I was done, all these older
scientists being nice people would would
want to encourage me, this young guy.
But when I got done, I stepped off the
podium and a whole bunch of them came up
to me and they said, "We don't believe
anything you just said." and they said
the molecules were too big and the
solvents we were using methylene
chloride and ethanol they'd destroy
whatever we put in um and actually
things went downhill for me for there I
my first nine grants uh to the NIH and
other places got rejected largely I
still remember one of the ones because
it was a cancer grant they said well Dr.
language engineer. He he knows nothing
about biology and even less about
oncology. I I thought mathematically
that was hard to do but nonetheless I I
didn't I didn't do too well. And then
you know I tried to you know I love
being a posttock but a lot of my friends
gave me advice. They said probably you
shouldn't do that forever. So I applied
to faculty positions and I applied to
chemical engineering departments because
that's my background. But actually no
chemical engineering department in the
world uh would offer me a job. They all
said well you want to do this bio stuff
you know you should be doing energy or
oil or mathematical modeling. So I
didn't get any jobs there. The reason I
got one finally is uh Nevin Scrimshaw
who was head of the nutrition department
uh he I don't know if
anybody knew Nevin but he was what I
call a benevolent dictator kind of
department head. And what I mean by that
is you know he liked me so he offered me
a job but he didn't ask anybody else in
the department what they
thought but which would have been okay
except for one thing which was that the
year after I joined the department he
left. So, so, so a lot of the senior
faculty decided to give me advice and
their advice is I I I should leave too
and and and and in fact number of years
ago I got some award and they asked Mike
Marleta who was one of my colleagues uh
at the time to give say a few words and
uh and and and and so he w he later
became uh uh chair at Berkeley head of
scripts. is a very famous scientist but
he he was giving a talk about uh about
what it was like to be with me in
1979 and what he said I'll just read it
he said this is direct quote uh they
even had published it was said one
evening I went to a faculty dinner at a
Chinese restaurant with myself and s MIT
professors he said a senior scientist
sat quizzing us while smoking a cigar he
said when the older scientist heard my
concepts for drug delivery he blew a
cloud of smoke in my face and said you
better start looking for another job. So
I I had a pretty inospicious beginning
but you know I I I had wonderful
students here and I kept trying and we
did actually with that that was the
essay that I mentioned and I'm and I um
I'm just trying to see if I u and and I
I have to apologize for this next
picture but there's no other way to show
blood vessels so I'll show it quickly
but we did actually use this essay and
we did actually publish in the journal
Science that inhibitors did exist and
I'll I'll show it very quickly, but what
you'll see is on the left, uh that's
what you normally get on the right, and
we did that over 2,000 tests. We had a
lots of controls because most of our
tests didn't most of the molecules we
tested didn't work. But when you do put
an inhibitor, and we repeated this 21
times, notice how the vessels are lower
and they're sparser. And um so
basically, I just want to turn that off
for a second. Um so so um so so that
actually showed for the first time that
blood vessels did inhibitors did exist
but it would also provide assays that
people could then use and continue to
use to this day to find out if they have
inhibitors or not. And so what would
happen you know medicine doesn't happen
quickly the translational part. So, so
we published that in
1976. It took another 28 years and
really terrific work out of Genanitech
and other places before the first blood
vessel inhibitor would get approved. But
starting in in 2004, so that was
actually the year we'd come something
remarkable would happen and I will just
show you that and what you see is
avasten gets approved actually that's
geniteex drug which is probably the
number two or three bestselling biotech
drug in history. But you see they keep
going even to even till last year and
they'll keep going. Nature the journal
estimates that half a billion people
will use these uh and and they're not
only the treatments for almost many
kinds of cancer they're really the only
treatments for diseases of blindness in
the back of the eye like macular
degeneration or diabetic retinopathy.
Now one other thing that I wanted to
mention which on translation is that you
know Dr. Dr. Folkman and I talked about
writing a patent and so you know at that
time at Children's Hospital never had a
patent uh and so we filed one for the
first time in five years in a row it got
rejected and I remember the techn
transfer people telling coming down to
see me and they said Bob you know you're
wasting so much money for the hospital
you just should quit and and uh but I
don't like to quit and I started to
think how could we convince the patent
examiner to allow this patent legally of
And science really wasn't working,
right? You know, so but I remembered as
I told you when I first started doing
this, everyone said it was impossible.
It could never work. I wondered maybe
somebody wrote that down. So you can do
something called a science citation
search, meaning you could look back at
who at your nature paper and see who
wrote about it. So now in
1982, you know, six years after that
came out and all after all these
rejections, I did that and I found well
I'll just show you one article uh but
this is from five of the top material
scientists in the world and they had
written this three years earlier. I had
no idea they wrote it but I'll just read
it to you. They were basically
describing this field and they said
generally the agent to be released as a
relatively large small molecule. Um, one
wouldn't expect that macroolelecules
could be released because of their
extremely small permeation rates.
However, Folkman and myself have
reported some surprising results that
clearly demonstrate the opposite. So, I
really like that word surprising and uh
and so we showed it to the people at the
hospital. They flew down to Washington
to show it to the examiner and he said,
"Gee," he had no idea. He said, "Tell
you what, I will allow this patent." He
said, "If Dr. Langanger can get written
affidavits from each of these five
people that they really wrote this. So
that it's a true story. I wrote them and
they were nice enough to write me back.
They really did write it. And um we got
this really broad patent. And with that
finally what happened was is I um you
know so I finally get calls from some of
these giant companies and they'd say,
"Well, we'd like you to consult for us."
And two two multi-billion dollar
companies, they they uh licensed the
patent. They gave me a grant and they
gave me a consulting fee and I was so
excited. I thought finally something I
did might make a difference in the
world. But what happened was after a
year or two, you know, they did some
experiments, didn't turn out exactly the
way they want, so they gave up. But then
Alex Kleinoff, one of my good friends
and professor at MIT at the time, said,
"Bob, we should start our own company."
So we did that. We started a little
company and we've kept doing that. And
I've gotten involved in a number of
translational things all from different
things we've done at MIT. And some of
them are based on our that those
companies, some on our patents, some
just on the science we've done. But the
consequence of that is now drug delivery
systems are used for like this based on
what we did. They're used for all kinds
of things really for actually billions
of of of people. uh in particular I mean
more recently uh one of the companies I
helped start which is based on
nanotechnology drug delivery systems is
is uh Madna but here's one that uh Phil
and I were both involved in which came
from El Nylm and and yet there's I hope
nobody has some of these but drug
alluding stances are widely used for
treating heart disease and many many
others here in fact from the
neuroscience standpoint a little bit you
know some of these uh some of these
treatments are end up using for uh
schizophrenia and and actually opioid
addiction. Uh so there are many of these
and we've continued to do um work on on
other areas as well from a basic science
standpoint. Uh and and one of the other
ones that we've been very involved in is
creating new tissues and organs from
scratch. And I put some of these on.
We've been and and these could provide
new tools including even doing trying to
create brains on a on a chip. But but
basically these are all things that uh
and many of these have now become real
treatments like making new skin for burn
victims and actually Nicholson one of my
students who went to Yale uh actually
created new way to make new blood
vessels that's now being used for
soldiers in the Ukraine and was just
approved by the FDA uh about two months
ago. The last thing I thought I'd try to
say is the point about collaboration.
you know, I mean, all this happened
because of so many great people at MIT
and elsewhere and anything that you do,
you know, which really ends up having
these effects is the work of many
people. So I thought I as Bob had
mentioned to me about collaboration, I
just went through actually looking at
the people who are on the McGovern
faculty that I've actually had the
pleasure of interacting with in
different ways and uh and and so
actually and and when I looked at this,
it's pretty much half the people uh I've
ended up working with in some way or
another. Some uh on on paper, some on
patents, some on boards and and others.
and uh these are all just the the things
that have come out from them and from
all of you and you know really have just
been absolutely remarkable. So for me
it's been an honor to work with the
different people at the McGovern to
watch and be a part of the board to see
the amazing work that's been done and I
just want to you know thank uh everybody
here for what they've done for the
McGovern and it's been an honor for me
to be present this today. Thanks so
much.
[Applause]
